body_txt: The Chinese tourist stereotype is in fact a relatively new development, thanks to China's recent economic prosperity allowing more citizens to travel the world, but nevertheless can be traced way back in history (see also Hordes from the East , in which Mongols were seen as this by the Westerners). During the Imperial Era, China was referred to as the "land of ceremony and etiquette"—concepts which were exported to (and can still be found to some extent in) neighboring countries like Mongolia, Korea and Japan, although these are generally not as rigorously applied there as they were in China. Traditional Chinese etiquette was born out of centuries-old Chinese religious practices and Confucian doctrine , which are notoriously multi-tiered systems . However, as China was being torn apart by Western powers and Japan during the New Culture Movement in the 1910s, there was a dedicated effort to "modernize" the country. The results were an overthrow of the monarchy, an attempt at simplifying the Chinese writing system, and the abandonment of many Chinese cultural elements such as highly formalized speech, traditional Chinese clothing, Chinese superstitions, and coming-of-age ceremonies. This was further exacerbated by the Communist Cultural Revolution, which denounced these rules as relics of feudalism, and this trope was born as a result. In the Western world, stereotypes about rude Chinese workers and shopkeepers date back to the 19th century, where thousands of poor Chinese men, facing a home country ravaged by war and economic decay, traveled to the United States and Australia to work as menial laborers building railroads or mining gold. When the gold dried up and railways finished, many Chinese workers took other forms of cheap labor that Westerners found demeaning, such as tending laundries inside local Chinatowns. Due to the fact that they spoke a completely different language with an alien writing system, understood little to no English, and wore distinct dresses and hairstyles (queue, a Manchu braid that was mandatory for all Chinese men), they were deemed as rude and incapable of assimilating into Western society. This unfortunately led to various forms of racial discrimination, from restrictive laws banning further Chinese immigration to the United States, to works of Yellow Peril depicting them as sinister and expansionist, and an affront to Western cultural norms. This in turn led to many Chinese store owners being overprotective of their businesses, and distrusting of non-Chinese customers and the occasional government inspector. The concept of course does not apply only to the Chinese—but given that China is the dominant regional culture (largely a result of it being the most populous country in East Asia and massive immigration to the Western world), and that Asian countries are often conflated in the Western world, the rest of East Asia has subsequently been associated with such rudeness. Japan is the major exception; their people are known for strict adherence to etiquette, respect, and politeness in all situations . Although this was very much averted historically with both the Chinese and Koreans viewing the Japanese as rude and backwards (not helped by the fact that both countries suffered from Japanese expansionism in WWII), and the Japanese have been often portrayed as the Proud Warrior Race Guys even in modern stories thanks to Cyberpunk . Some of this perceived "rudeness" comes from a linguistic Cross-Cultural Kerfluffle —speakers of Chinese and many East and Southeast Asian languages often find it natural to speak with a kind of pitch and tone contour that can come across as a bit strident or hectoring to speakers of non-tonal languages. Differing cultural notions of what is rude and what isn't don't help, either; Chinese Etiquette can be as foreign as the language, and may even make one come off as an Etiquette Nazi , while what are seen as cultural norms in China and other East Asian countries, such as obedience to one's elders , tiger parenting , and strict child discipline , can be deemed as unnecessarily authoritarian or even child abuse in the Western world. This trope is often Played for Laughs (with cultural posturing all too often a part of such humor), but can also be Played for Drama , especially among children of Asian immigrants growing up among these clashing cultures. It might even cause Stop Being Stereotypical . Can be seen in an Asian Store-Owner and can overlap with Arrogant Kung-Fu Guy , or (in Japanese media) Bokukko or even The Idiot from Osaka . Common Western equivalents include the French Jerk and the "Ugly American" Stereotype , the American tourist (often in a Hawaiian shirt ), businessperson, or bureaucrat who exhibits all the worst traits of the boorish variant of Eagleland when traveling abroad. Contrast Japanese Politeness (although Japanese people are not always portrayed as polite) and Inscrutable Oriental (where the Asian is more of a pompous know-it-all). noreallife
